coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criterion for ADF map relocation destination with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criterion for ADF map relocation destination

coaching.coaching: Realign reconcile_check.py as an importer-site DI target to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.
coaching.subject: reconcile_check.py as an importer-site DI target

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that adf/comment_limits sizing constants and value-maps are present in adapters/jira (replacement side of the migration), not just that the old imports are removed.
evidence: Approach: 'Relocate _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK + ADF-sizing/value-maps into adapters/jira single-source'
AC1 covers only removal: 'no longer import outbound_fields/inbound_fields/adf/comment_limits directly'
AC2 covers only _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK: 'outbound_links._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK single-sourced under adapters/jira'
No AC states that adf-sizing/value-maps now live in adapters/jira
findings.finding: The Approach section describes relocating 'ADF-sizing/value-maps into adapters/jira single-source' alongside _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK, but no acceptance criterion verifies the replacement destination of adf/comment_limits content — AC1 only verifies removal (no longer import adf/comment_limits directly), leaving the 'replacement' half of this migration uncovered.
findings.location: Approach section ('Relocate _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK + ADF-sizing/value-maps into adapters/jira single-source') vs AC1 and AC2
scenarios: An implementer deletes the adf/comment_limits imports without moving the constants anywhere — all ACs pass but the relocation deliverable is incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] adf/comment_limits sizing constants and value-maps are single-sourced under adapters/jira; no duplicated definitions remain in core modules.' as a new AC line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `reconcile_check.py` to the plan's file-impact list and confirm whether its direct loads of `outbound_fields._extract_jira_field`/`_assignee_matches` and `adf.fit_text_to_adf_limit` need updating if those symbols move during the refactor.
evidence: reconcile_check.py:61-75: defines _load_outbound_fields() returning _load_sibling('outbound_fields', 'adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py') and _load_adf() returning _load_sibling('adf', 'adapters/jira/adf.py')
reconcile_check.py:225: 'of = _load_outbound_fields()' — uses _extract_jira_field, _assignee_matches from outbound_fields
reconcile_check.py is not listed in the plan's Approach section or any file impact list
findings.finding: reconcile_check.py is an unenumerated consumer of adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py (imports _extract_jira_field, _assignee_matches via _load_outbound_fields()) and adapters/jira/adf.py (imports fit_text_to_adf_limit via _load_adf()). If the DI refactor renames or restructures these helper functions as part of moving logic into adapters/jira/, reconcile_check.py must be updated too, but it appears nowhere in the plan's file list or scope.
scenarios: If outbound_fields._extract_jira_field or _assignee_matches move/rename as part of the DI refactor, reconcile_check breaks silently because it loads by file path (not package import), so import errors only surface at runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `reconcile_check.py` to the leaf's scope (AC1 and 'Remaining work'): remove its `_load_sibling outbound_fields/adf` imports and route them through the injected Backend mapper, as the parent's file_impact specifies.
evidence: Parent file_impact entry: {"path": "src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile_check.py", "reason": "Drop _load_sibling outbound_fields/adf (:70,:75); use Backend"}
Leaf 'Remaining work': lists outbound_differ.py, inbound_differ.py, apply_inbound.py, outbound_comments.py, inbound_translate.py, outbound_links.py — reconcile_check.py is absent

Leaf AC1: 'outbound_differ/inbound_differ/apply_inbound/outbound_comments/inbound_translate no longer import outbound_fields/inbound_fields/adf/comment_limits directly' — reconcile_check.py not listed
findings.finding: The leaf plan omits reconcile_check.py from its scope, even though the parent explicitly assigned it to this split as an importer-site DI fix ('Drop _load_sibling outbound_fields/adf (:70,:75); use Backend' in file_impact). The leaf's AC1 and 'Remaining work' section list only five modules (outbound_differ, inbound_differ, apply_inbound, outbound_comments, inbound_translate), silently dropping a parent-declared deliverable.
findings.location: AC1 / 'Remaining work' section of leaf plan
scenarios: reconcile_check.py retains its direct _load_sibling outbound_fields/adf imports after this leaf lands, leaving a vendor-mapper import leak in the core that the parent's file_impact explicitly targeted for removal.

The neutrality gate extension ('no differ/apply core module imports the vendor mappers') may not cover reconcile_check.py if that module is not included in scope, allowing the leak to pass the gate undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Remaining work' section, add: '- reconcile_check.py (_load_sibling outbound_fields/adf at :70,:75)'. In AC1, extend the list to include reconcile_check.py. Ensure the neutrality gate also asserts no vendor-mapper import in reconcile_check.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the chosen injection shape explicitly in the Approach section (e.g. 'pass `backend.outbound` and `backend.inbound` as discrete mapper parameters, NOT the whole Backend object') so the executor has a single unambiguous signature contract to implement.
evidence: 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter into compute_outbound_mutations / the inbound differ / apply_inbound'
AC1 says 'they receive the mapper via injection' but does not constrain what the injected type is
The choice affects how many parameters are added per function and what callers must supply
findings.finding: The Approach section says 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter' — the 'or' leaves the actual injection shape (whole Backend object vs individual mapper fields) as an unresolved design choice deferred to the executor. Injecting a full Backend has different implications for function signatures, call sites in run_differs/reconcile, and test double construction than injecting discrete mapper fields.
findings.location: Approach section, sentence: 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter'
scenarios: An executor who injects the whole Backend object will produce different call-site and test signatures than one who injects individual mapper sub-objects, making review difficult and possibly forcing a re-do
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper)' with the decided form, e.g.: 'Pass `backend.outbound`, `backend.inbound`, and `backend.sanitizer` as discrete positional-or-keyword parameters to compute_outbound_mutations, the inbound differ, and apply_inbound respectively; callers (run_differs/reconcile) obtain these from select_backend(load_config()) once and thread them down.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g. a feature-flag, a revert-commit strategy, or an expand-contract sequencing note) for the signature refactor of compute_outbound_mutations/inbound differ/apply_inbound.
evidence: Approach: 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter into compute_outbound_mutations / the inbound differ / apply_inbound'
'Why deferred': 'a signature refactor across the differ/apply modules and their tests'
No rollback or back-out step is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a signature refactor on compute_outbound_mutations / inbound differ / apply_inbound (adding a mapper/Backend parameter) — a breaking change to existing call sites — but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan if the refactor causes regressions beyond what the test suite can catch (e.g. integration failures discovered post-merge).
findings.location: Approach section; 'Why deferred / the constraint' section
scenarios: A regression surfaces post-merge in an integration environment not fully covered by the unit suite; without a rollback plan the team must hotfix forward under pressure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the injection unit across sections: either confirm that only the individual mapper (backend.outbound/inbound/sanitizer) is injected (satisfying the isolation constraint) or explicitly justify why injecting the full Backend object is acceptable given the unit-test isolation requirement.
evidence: Why deferred: 'the differ functions must receive the mapper (backend.outbound/inbound/sanitizer) by DEPENDENCY INJECTION'
Approach: 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter'

If a Backend object is passed, unit tests cannot isolate mapping logic without constructing a full Backend; if only mapper is passed, isolation is preserved — the two options are not equivalent under the stated constraint.
findings.finding: The 'Why deferred / the constraint' section resolves clearly to mapper injection ('the differ functions must receive the mapper (backend.outbound/inbound/sanitizer) by DEPENDENCY INJECTION'), but the Approach section re-opens the question with 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper)' — these two sections give the implementer contradictory design signals about the injection unit (whole Backend object vs. individual mapper).
findings.location: 'Why deferred / the constraint' section vs Approach section
scenarios: An implementer chooses to inject the full Backend object (arguably permitted by Approach), but the unit-test isolation constraint (Why deferred) is then violated, since tests would need a complete Backend stub rather than a lightweight mapper stub.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper)' with 'Thread the relevant mapper (backend.outbound, backend.inbound, or backend.sanitizer as appropriate)' to match the constraint section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update AGENTS.md / an ADR / design doc to capture the mapper-injection DI contract: which layer owns Backend instantiation, which functions receive the mapper parameter, and the neutrality invariant these enforce.
evidence: 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter into compute_outbound_mutations / the inbound differ / apply_inbound, obtained once by the core orchestrator'
No mention of AGENTS.md update, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
S4 is referenced as having 'landed the transport-seam half' — this ticket completes the pattern, making documentation of the full DI contract now appropriate
findings.finding: The plan changes the public signatures of core orchestration functions (compute_outbound_mutations, inbound differ, apply_inbound) and establishes a new DI contract for mapper injection, but does not mention updating AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design doc to capture this evolved architectural pattern for future contributors.
scenarios: A new contributor adds a third differ module and unknowingly imports a vendor mapper directly (violating the neutrality contract) because the DI convention is only discoverable by reading the neutrality gate test, not any documented architectural decision
The Backend parameter threading pattern diverges across callers because the expected injection point (orchestrator vs. caller) is not documented
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief section to AGENTS.md (or a new ADR) under the reconciler architecture covering: (1) the Backend DI boundary — core differ/apply modules receive mapper objects as parameters, never import vendor modules directly; (2) the orchestrator (run_differs/reconcile) is the sole site that calls select_backend(); (3) the neutrality gate asserts this invariant. Reference both S4 and this ticket as the origin.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` has NO existing definition in `acli_graph.py`; the plan should state it needs to be CREATED (not merely re-referenced) under `adapters/jira/` (e.g., in `acli_graph.py` or a new constants module), and update AC-2 accordingly.
evidence: Plan states: 'outbound_links.py holds _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Jira link-relation constant) in backend-neutral core'
outbound_links.py:30: '# acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1), but the differ is loaded standalone...'
Search of adapters/jira/acli_graph.py finds zero occurrences of '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK'
The only definition of _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK in the entire codebase is outbound_links.py:42
findings.finding: The plan (and the outbound_links.py comment it relies on) claims _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK's 'canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)', but this symbol does not exist in adapters/jira/acli_graph.py. The only definition is the copy in outbound_links.py itself. AC-2 ('outbound_links._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK single-sourced under adapters/jira') targets moving a symbol whose claimed upstream canonical source is absent, meaning the implementer may search for a non-existent reference point.
scenarios: Implementer looks for the 'acli_graph canonical definition' to reference or remove it — it does not exist, causing confusion about where to move the constant to and whether a duplicate needs deduplication.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the plan to say: 'Define _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK in adapters/jira/acli_graph.py (or a dedicated adapters/jira/link_constants.py) for the first time as the single source of truth; remove it from outbound_links.py and import it from there.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan an explicit strategy for how outbound_links.py will import the relocated _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK in a spec_from_file_location-compatible way (e.g., use the existing lazy_load pattern, or inline a small lazy bootstrap identical to how adf/comment_limits are handled).
evidence: outbound_links.py:30-35: explicitly explains the current re-declaration exists BECAUSE 'the differ is loaded standalone via spec_from_file_location in tests (no package context, so from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.acli_graph import ... is not reliably importable)'
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_link_dedup.py:36: spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(name, path) — loads outbound_differ.py which imports from outbound_links
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_link_removal_sync.py:34-35: loads outbound_differ.py standalone
Plan does not describe a lazy_load pattern or other workaround for outbound_links.py's cross-package import after relocation
findings.finding: outbound_links.py is loaded standalone via spec_from_file_location in test_link_dedup.py and test_link_removal_sync.py. When _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK moves to adapters/jira/, outbound_links.py will need to import it from there — but this re-creates the original standalone-load problem for outbound_links.py (a plain package import from adapters/jira/ will fail under spec_from_file_location without package context). The plan does not address how outbound_links.py will reference the relocated constant in a spec-load-compatible way.
scenarios: After relocation, outbound_links.py does 'from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira import link_constants' — this fails when outbound_links is spec-loaded standalone in unit tests, breaking the entire link-diff test suite.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the same lazy_load bootstrap pattern already used in outbound_links.py's sibling outbound_comments.py for adf/comment_limits: load the adapters/jira constants module by file path via lazy_load(_KEY, 'adapters/jira/acli_graph.py') rather than a plain package import.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Approach whether the injected parameter type is the full `Backend` or only the relevant role sub-object (e.g. `OutboundMapper` into `compute_outbound_mutations`, `InboundMapper` into the inbound differ, `FieldSanitizer` into the comment/ADF paths) — the role-sub-object form is consistent with the Backend port's interface-segregation design.
evidence: 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter into compute_outbound_mutations / the inbound differ / apply_inbound'
_backend.py defines five distinct role Protocols (OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention, TicketTransport) specifically to enable narrow injection
The existing OutboundDiffConfig pattern in run_differs already injects specific fields (client, pass_id, etc.) rather than a monolithic context object
findings.finding: The Approach section says 'Thread a Backend (or its outbound/inbound/sanitizer mapper) as a parameter' without resolving the ambiguity: passing the full Backend object couples core differ/apply modules to the entire Backend protocol surface (including transport, identity, capability flags), while passing only the role sub-objects (outbound, inbound, sanitizer) respects the port's interface-segregation intent established in _backend.py. The implementer must choose, and the wrong choice (full Backend everywhere) would partially reintroduce the coupling this story aims to remove.
scenarios: Implementer passes the full Backend to compute_outbound_mutations, which then accesses backend.transport inside the differ — reintroducing a transport dependency in the core differ.
The neutrality gate cannot distinguish 'full Backend imported' from 'role object injected' without specifying which Protocol is the injection type.
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the Approach sentence to: 'Thread only the relevant role sub-object (OutboundMapper into compute_outbound_mutations, InboundMapper into the inbound differ, FieldSanitizer into the ADF/comment-limit paths) as a parameter, obtained once by the orchestrator via select_backend(load_config()) and injected at the call site — never the full Backend object.' This matches the port's interface-segregation intent and keeps test doubles minimal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Approach how outbound_links.py will access the relocated constant after it moves to adapters/jira — either inject it as a parameter (consistent with the DI approach) or confirm the relocation uses a neutrality-safe accessor that does not create a new static core→adapter import.
evidence: 'outbound_links._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Jira link-relation constant) in backend-neutral core'
'Relocate _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK + ADF-sizing/value-maps into adapters/jira single-source'

Existing outbound_links.py comment (lines 30-35): 'the canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1), but the differ is loaded standalone via spec_from_file_location in tests... We re-declare a local copy here'
The neutrality gate (test_backend_neutrality.py) currently only covers AcliClient construction and jira-literal sites; it would need extension to cover outbound_links imports
findings.finding: The plan says 'Relocate _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK + ADF-sizing/value-maps into adapters/jira single-source' but does not specify HOW outbound_links.py (a core module) will subsequently access the constant. Today, the existing comment in outbound_links.py explicitly explains it re-declares the constant locally BECAUSE a direct import from adapters/jira would 'pull the whole ACLI client import chain' in spec_from_file_location test contexts. After relocation, outbound_links.py must either (a) import from adapters/jira (creating a new core→adapter static import that the neutrality gate should block), or (b) receive the constant via mapper injection (consistent with the DI approach). The plan leaves this unresolved, creating a risk that the relocation creates a new neutrality violation instead of eliminating one.
scenarios: Implementer moves the constant to adapters/jira/jira_fields.py and adds 'from rebar_reconciler.adapters.jira.jira_fields import _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' in outbound_links.py — creating the exact new core→adapter import the neutrality story aims to prevent.
The link-diff tests that load outbound_links.py standalone fail because the adapters/jira import chain pulls AcliClient.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
